Austria: In the questionnaire the Bundesamt für Eich- und Vermessungswen (BEV) who is the responsible mapping agency in Austria responded that no national point cloud or point cloud elevation model is available. Therefore, the data acquisition of Austria is more focused on the different regions in the county.
Flanders: For Flanders this dataset is called the Digitaal Hoogtemodel Vlaanderen (DHV). The point cloud dataset is acquired by ALS and is divided into different NGI kaartbladen, with 43 smaller areas. Such that the point cloud dataset of Flanders is a harmonised point cloud dataset from the same acquisition platform. (Agentschap voor Geografische Informatie Vlaanderen (AGIV), 2017)
Wallonia: The point cloud data set of Wallonia is a provincial dataset (Service public de Wallonie (SPW), 2023). The point cloud dataset was acquired by ALS and is divided into 6 different blocks. Such that the point cloud dataset of Wallonia is a harmonised point cloud dataset from the same acquisition platform. The point cloud dataset is available in LAS 1.2-1.4 and can be downloaded in LAZ (Dominique Buffet and Christophe Schenke and Christophe Domine, 2014).
Brussels: The point cloud dataset of Brussel was acquired in April 2012 and October 2021. The data was acquired to create a city model, initially for a LoD2 and the second acquisition to build a digital twin of the city (Perello, 2024), (Brussels-Capital Region, 2023).
Czechia: The Czech Republic does not have a point cloud dataset freely accessible to the public, only the ALS derived digital elevation model. The Digitální model reliéfu České republiky (DRM) is available in different generations e.g. 4th (4G) and 5th generation (5G). Based on the DMR 5G quality rapport, the LiDAR acquisition was conducted at an elevation of 1200m and 1400m (Leitmannová, Gálová, & Michalík, 2017).The elevation model has an altimetric error between 0.18m and 0.3m, depending on the type of terrain. The DMR 5G dataset was acquired between 2009 and 2013 and was published on 30 of June 2016. While the data has an update cycle, with the latest acquisition campaigns between 2020 and 2025. There is no information about the classification of the digital elevation model.
Denmark: The national point cloud acquisition campaign of Denmark is the ’Danmarks Hojdemodel (DHM)’. The dataset is available as open data; however, you need to register to download the data through the Danish geo portal. The acquisition is conducted at five-year intervals, covering 1/5 of Denmark each year such that the point cloud is acquired in 5 blocks and is integrated in one national dataset (Danish Agency for Data Supply and Efficiency, 2020). The point cloud of the DHM (product name: Punktsky) is available according to the las specifications 1.4, where the LAZ data structure is used to distribute the data. The point cloud is referenced to zone 32 of the ETRS89 (EPSG: 25832), with the vertical reference system DVR90.
Germany: In Germany, the availability of point cloud datasets is divided between the federal government and the Bundesländer (federal states), reflecting a distinction between national and regional scopes. Several datasets were identified in Germany that are available as open-access point cloud data. All elevation or point cloud datasets are acquired using the ETRS89 / UTM Zone 32 or 33 coordinate systems. For vertical reference, data acquired before 2017 use the DHHN92 height system, while data acquired from 2017 onwards use DHHN2016.
